1: “Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook?” — the opening challenge of the Leviathan speech. Matthew 12:29: “how can someone enter a strong man’s house and plunder his goods, unless he first binds the strong man?” Leviathan, the primordial chaos-serpent, is the “strong man” that no human fisher can hook. Christ is the only one who can draw out and bind Leviathan — not through superior creaturely force but through the victory of the cross (Colossians 2:15: “disarming the rulers and authorities and putting them to open shame, triumphing over them in him”).
2: Can you put a rope in his nose or pierce his jaw with a hook? — the impossibility of controlling the sea-monster. Isaiah 27:1: “in that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea.” The hook that no creature can drive through Leviathan’s jaw is wielded by YHWH in the eschatological day; in the NT this is identified with Christ’s final victory: Revelation 20:2 (“he seized the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan, and bound him”).
3: Will Leviathan speak with soft words to you, will he make a covenant with you? — the impossibility of diplomacy with the chaos-monster. Hebrews 2:14: “since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil.” Leviathan does not make covenants on human terms; Christ did not negotiate with death but destroyed it by undergoing it. The new covenant that Christ makes is not a diplomatic arrangement with the serpent but the serpent’s defeat.
4: Will he make a covenant with you to be taken as a servant forever? — the impossibility of taming Leviathan for perpetual service. Revelation 20:3: Satan “bound…so that he might not deceive the nations any longer, until the thousand years were ended.” The permanent taming of Leviathan that no human can accomplish is the accomplished fact of Christ’s victory: the ancient serpent will be not only bound but cast into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10), permanently removed from his role as deceiver.
5: Will you play with Leviathan as with a bird, or put him on a leash for your girls? — the absurdity of treating the chaos-monster as a pet. Colossians 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.” The enemies of creation that could never be made into pets are put to open shame by the cross: their power is publicly displayed as defeated. What appears to be Leviathan’s triumph at the cross is the place where Christ “put them to open shame.”
6: Will traders make a bargain for him, will they divide him up among the merchants? — the economic impossibility of commercializing the chaos-monster. 1 Peter 1:18–19: “you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.” No merchant can bargain for Leviathan; but Christ paid the ransom that no commercial transaction could accomplish — not silver or gold but his own blood.
7: Can you fill his hide with harpoons or his head with fish spears? — the piercing of the impenetrable hide. Zechariah 12:10: “they will look on me, on him whom they have pierced.” John 19:34: “one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and at once blood and water came out.” The impenetrable Leviathan whom no spear can pierce is the anti-type of Christ whose side was pierced — the one who was vulnerable to the very weapon that cannot reach the chaos-serpent. Christ absorbed the wound that Leviathan is immune to.
8: “Lay your hands on him; remember the battle; you will not do it again!” — the fearful warning against trying to fight Leviathan. Hebrews 2:14–15: “that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death…and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.” Those enslaved by the fear of death-in-battle with Leviathan — never daring to lay hands on him — are set free by Christ who fought and destroyed death on their behalf. The warning of v8 is the freedom of those liberated by Christ’s vicarious battle.
9: Behold, any hope of overcoming Leviathan is false; at the sight of him one is cast down — the hopelessness of the creature before the chaos-monster. Romans 7:24: “wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?” The hopelessness that any human faces against Leviathan is the spiritual despair of the one who cannot defeat the power of sin and death through self-effort. The answer: v25: “thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!” — the deliverer who overcomes where all human hope is false.
10: No one is so fierce that he would dare to stir up Leviathan — the universal creaturely fear. Revelation 5:3–5: “no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth was able to open the scroll…the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered.” The scroll that no creature can open and the Leviathan that no creature dares stir are the same problem: the deficiency of all creaturely power before the ultimate challenge. The Lamb who takes the scroll is the Christ who alone dared to stir Leviathan — and conquered.
11: “Who has first given to me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under the whole heaven is mine” — the divine ownership of all things, pre-empting any human claim on God. Romans 11:35–36: “or who has given a gift to him that he might be repaid? For from him and through him and to him are all things.” Paul quotes this very logic in his doxology concluding the argument for divine sovereignty in salvation. The divine ownership that YHWH asserts over Leviathan is the ownership that Christ shares: John 17:10: “all mine are yours, and yours are mine.”
12: “I will not keep silence concerning his limbs, or his mighty strength, or his goodly frame” — YHWH speaking fully about Leviathan’s design. John 1:1: “in the beginning was the Word.” The divine speech that brings Leviathan’s frame into existence and describes it is the same divine Word through whom all things were made (John 1:3). The Creator who speaks Leviathan’s limbs into being is the Logos; the thorough divine description of the chaos-monster is itself an act of sovereign lordship over it.
13: Who can strip off his outer garment, who can penetrate his double coat of mail? — the impenetrable armor of Leviathan. Ephesians 6:11–13: “put on the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil.” The armor that covers the enemy and makes him invulnerable to creaturely attack is defeated by Christ’s own armor of divine righteousness. The spiritual armor of God (Job 6:14–17) is the equipment Christ provides to stand against the Leviathan-enemy whose own armor no human penetrates.
14: Who can open the doors of his face, around his teeth is terror — the terrifying mouth of Leviathan. Revelation 12:9: “the great dragon was thrown down, that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world.” Revelation 20:2: “he seized the dragon, that ancient serpent.” The terrifying mouth of the chaos-serpent is the mouth of the dragon whom Christ, the one who holds “the keys of Death and Hades” (Revelation 1:18), has authority to bind and cast into the lake of fire.
15: His back is made of rows of shields, shut up closely as with a seal — the sealed armor of Leviathan. Matthew 27:66: the tomb of Christ was “made secure by sealing the stone and setting a guard.” The sealed armor that no creature can penetrate is the ironic type of the sealed tomb that could not hold Christ. The same sealing that makes Leviathan impenetrable made the tomb “secure” — and Christ passed through it in resurrection. The seal that defends Leviathan became the seal that Christ defeated on Easter morning.
16: Each of them is joined to the next; no air can come between them — the seamless armor of the chaos-monster. John 19:23–24: Christ’s tunic was “seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom.” The seamless tunic that the soldiers would not divide by lot is a type of Christ’s priesthood: Hebrews 7:24 (“he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever”). The seamlessness of Leviathan’s armor is the grotesque anti-type of Christ’s unbroken, undivided priesthood and perfect integrity.
17: They are joined one to another; they clasp each other and cannot be separated — the inseparability of Leviathan’s armor. Romans 8:38–39: “neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers…will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.” The inseparable union of Leviathan’s scales is the anti-type of the inseparable union between Christ and those who are his. What holds Leviathan together in menace, holds believers in safety: nothing can separate from Christ’s love.
18: His sneezings flash forth light, and his eyes are like the eyelids of the dawn — Leviathan’s eyes like the dawn. Revelation 1:16: “his face was like the sun shining in full strength.” Revelation 2:28; Job 22:16: Christ as “the bright morning star.” Leviathan’s dawn-eyes are the terrible parody of Christ’s radiant face and morning-star identity. The light associated with the chaos-monster is the counterfeit of the light that Christ is (John 8:12: “I am the light of the world”).
19: Out of his mouth go flaming torches; sparks of fire leap out — the fire-breathing of Leviathan. Acts 2:3: “divided tongues as of fire appeared to them and rested on each one of them.” The fire from Leviathan’s mouth is the destructive anti-type of the Spirit’s fire at Pentecost. Both are fire from a mouth; the first destroys, the second purifies and empowers. Christ sends the Spirit’s fire not as Leviathan breathes destructive flame but as the Baptizer with the Holy Spirit and fire (Matthew 3:11).
20: Out of his nostrils comes smoke as from a boiling pot and burning rushes — the smoke and steam of Leviathan. Revelation 9:2–3: smoke from the pit darkening the sun; the locust-horses with smoke from their mouths. The smoke of the chaos-monster becomes, in Revelation’s eschatological vision, the smoke of the abyss — demonic powers released for the final judgment. Revelation 8:4: “the smoke of the incense, with the prayers of the saints, rose before God from the hand of the angel.” The smoke of Leviathan has its countertype in the incense-smoke of the saints’ prayers ascending to God through Christ’s high-priestly intercession.
21: His breath kindles coals, and a flame comes forth from his mouth — Leviathan as living fire-source. Hebrews 12:29: “our God is a consuming fire.” The fire that belongs to God is the fire from which Leviathan’s breath is derived: the chaos-monster has a derived, creaturely version of the divine fire. Christ, who is one with the Father (John 10:30), participates in the divine fire that is ultimately not destructive for those in him but purifying: “he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire” (Matthew 3:11).
22: In his neck abides strength, and terror dances before him — the power and terror of Leviathan. Colossians 1:13: “he has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son.” The terror that dances before Leviathan is the terror of the domain of darkness from which Christ transfers those who are his. The power in Leviathan’s neck is the power of the dark domain; Christ’s kingdom transfer removes believers from that terror’s dominion.
23: The folds of his flesh stick together, firm on him and immovable — Leviathan’s impenetrable body. John 10:18: “no one takes [my life] from me, but I lay it down of my own accord.” The immovable flesh of Leviathan is the grotesque anti-type of Christ’s voluntary self-offering. Leviathan cannot be moved because of impenetrable power; Christ cannot be taken against his will because of sovereign love. Both are “immovable” in their flesh, but for opposite reasons — one from creaturely armor, one from divine sovereign love.
24: His heart is hard as stone, hard as the lower millstone — the stone-hard heart of the chaos-monster. Ezekiel 36:26: “I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh.” The stone-heart of Leviathan is the ultimate expression of the stone-heart that God removes through the new covenant. Christ’s crucified heart — pierced by a soldier’s spear (John 19:34), yielding blood and water (the signs of the sacraments) — is the opposite of Leviathan’s stone-heart.
25: When he raises himself up the mighty are afraid; at the crashing they are beside themselves — the universal fear of Leviathan. Revelation 6:15–16: at the wrath of the Lamb, “the kings of the earth and the great ones and the generals and the rich and the powerful, and everyone, slave and free, hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains.” The terror that Leviathan’s rising produces is the terror that the day of Christ’s wrath will produce — universal, without class distinction, overwhelming even the mighty.
26: Though the sword reaches him it does not avail; nor the spear, the dart, or the javelin — the weapons that cannot harm Leviathan. Ephesians 6:16: “the shield of faith, with which you can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one.” The weapons that cannot reach Leviathan have their countertype in the flaming darts of the enemy that Christ’s armor absorbs. The armor of God (Job 6:13–17) is the specific equipment that transforms the helplessness before Leviathan’s weapon-immunity into a protected ability to stand.
27: He counts iron as straw and bronze as rotten wood — the contempt Leviathan has for weapons. 1 Corinthians 1:18: “the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.” The weapons that Leviathan dismisses as straw are the weapons that Christ’s opponents dismiss — the apparent weakness of the cross. But the cross that looks like straw to worldly power is the weapon that actually defeats the ultimate enemy.
28: The arrow cannot make Leviathan flee; slingstones turn to stubble for him — projectile weapons failing against the monster. Luke 10:19: “behold, I have given you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy, and nothing shall hurt you.” The disciples’ authority over serpents and the enemy’s power is the delegated authority of Christ who overcame the ultimate serpent. The arrows that fail against Leviathan fail in human hands; in Christ’s authority, the power of the enemy is overcome.
29: Clubs are counted as stubble; he laughs at the rattle of javelins — Leviathan’s contemptuous power. Luke 11:22: “when one stronger than he attacks him and overcomes him, he takes away his armor in which he trusted.” Leviathan laughs at the javelins of the weak; the stronger one who overcomes him does not come with superior javelins but with a different kind of power — the cross and resurrection. The armor in which the strong man (Leviathan/the devil) trusted is taken away not by bigger weapons but by Christ’s unique victory.
30: His underparts are like sharp potsherds; he spreads himself like a threshing sledge on the mire — Leviathan leaving destruction in his wake. Isaiah 41:15: “behold, I make of you a threshing sledge, new, sharp, and having teeth; you shall thresh the mountains and crush them.” Isaiah applies the threshing-sledge image to Israel; Christ applies the threshing motif to himself: John 15:2 (“every branch that does bear fruit he prunes”). The destructive wake of Leviathan has its redemptive countertype in Christ’s pruning that produces fruit.
31: He makes the deep boil like a pot; he makes the sea like a pot of ointment — Leviathan churning the sea. Revelation 21:1: “the sea was no more.” The boiling sea that Leviathan stirs is permanently calmed in the new creation. Christ’s first display of authority over the sea (Matthew 8:26–27: “even the winds and the sea obey him”) is the firstfruits of the new creation’s perfect calm. The sea Leviathan boils is the sea Christ calms; the new creation’s “sea was no more” is the final calming after Leviathan’s permanent defeat.
32: Behind him he leaves a shining wake; one would think the deep had white hair — Leviathan’s luminous trail. Revelation 1:14: “the hairs of his head were white, like white wool, like snow.” The white-haired Ancient of Days imagery in Revelation belongs to the risen Christ. Leviathan’s white wake in the deep sea is the subverted type of Christ’s white-haired glory in the vision of John. What Leviathan’s passage leaves as a churning wake, Christ’s resurrection leaves as permanent transformed reality.
33: “On earth there is not his like, a creature without fear” — Leviathan’s incomparability on earth. Colossians 1:18–19: “he is the head of the body, the church. He is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in everything he might be preeminent. For in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell.” The incomparable creature on earth has its countertype in the incomparable Christ who is not merely the greatest creature but the fullness of God dwelling in creaturely form. Leviathan has no equal among creatures; Christ is the Creator who became a creature.
34: “He sees everything that is high; he is king over all the sons of pride” — Leviathan as the king of the proud. Revelation 19:16: “on his robe and on his thigh he has a name written, King of kings and Lord of lords.” The king of the sons of pride is dethroned by the King of kings. 1 Corinthians 15:25: “he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet.” The final enemy (death) is put under Christ’s feet after the cross and resurrection; Leviathan’s kingship over pride is superseded by Christ’s kingship over all — the kingdom given to him by the Father (1 Corinthians 15:28).
